case_name: Note 1   The possibility was raised, in expert evidence, that this may have been gas generated by bacteria, rather than air; but air was thought more likely; and, as we shall see, the consensus ultimately was that it was not a relevant feature for the purposes of what I had to decide.[Back]Note 2   In the course of evidence, reference was frequently made to a table showing the multiple, often overlapping, pathiophysiological mechanisms and pathways by which meningeal infection may lead to increased ICP, appearing in Tariq et al, The Role of IPC monitoring in meningitis,Neurological Focus(2017) 43, 1.  Though reference to this table helped the experts to explain their evidence, the issues in this case do not require me to descend to the same level of fine-grained detail.[Back]Note 3   Køster-Rasmussen et al Antibiotic treatment delay and outcome in acute bacterial meningitis,Journal of Infection(2008) 57, 449; Bodilsen et al, Time to antibiotic therapy and outcome in bacterial meningitis: a Danish population-based cohort study,BMC Infectious Diseases(2016) 16:392; Proulx et al, Delays in the administration of antibiotics are associated with mortality from adult acute bacterial meningitis,Q J Med(2005) 98, 291.[Back]Note 4   Schutte et al, A Prospective Study of Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS), Age, CSF-Neutrophil Count, and CSF-Protein and Glucose Levels as Prognostic Indicators in 100 Adult Patients with Meningitis,Journal of Infection(1998) 37, 112.[Back]Note 5   Durand et al, Acute Bacterial Meningitis in Adults,The New England Journal of Medicine(1993) 328, 21.[Back]Note 6   Østergaard et al, Clinical presentation and prognostic factors of Streptococcus pneumoniae meningitis according the focus of infection,BMC Infectious Diseases(2005) 5:93.[Back]Note 7   Frans Albers, Complications of Otitis Media,The American Journal of Otology(1999) 20: 9.[Back]Note 8   Auburtin et al Detrimental role of delayed antibiotic administration and penicillin-nonsusceptible strains in adult intensive care unit patients with pneumococcal meningitis: The PNEUMOREA prospective multicenter study,Critical Care Medicine(2006) 34, 2758.[Back]Note 9   Underlining in the original.[Back]Note 10   Note 5 above.[Back]Note 11   See note 1 above.[Back]Note 12   It appears that, as this was an application ofFairchildto a non-mesothelioma case, the Compensation Act 2006 did not apply, so the approach to compensation inBarker v Corus (UK) plcapplied.[Back]Note 13   Though with one party being the source of both causes.[Back]

summary.brief: Note 1   The possibility was raised, in expert evidence, that this may have been gas generated by bacteria, rather than air; but air was thought more likely; and, as we shall see, the consensus ultimately was that it was not a relevant feature for the purposes of what I had to decide.[Back]Note 2   In the course of evidence, reference was frequently made to a table showing the multiple, often overlapping, pathiophysiological mechanisms and pathways by which meningeal infection may lead to increa

evidence_index.text: On the morning of 25 February 2015 Mrs Davies was taken by ambulance from her home to Wexham Park Hospital in Slough. On the evening of 27 February she was declared dead.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Administration of intravenous (IV) antibiotics to Mrs Davies began at 13.20 on the day of hospital admission, 25 February 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: By the time that the matter came to trial it had been admitted and agreed that the Defendant was negligent by failing to begin administering antibiotics by 10.40 that day.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: At 10.10 Mrs Davies was prescribed the anti-sickness medicine Cyclizine and morphine sulphate for the pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Professor Masterton opined that on admission it was acceptable for Mrs Davies to be managed as an uncomplicated sepsis case, but nevertheless a delay of 4 hours and 10 minutes was unacceptable.
evidence_index.speaker: Professor Masterton

evidence_index.text: Mr Morrison concluded: 'Based on personal experience, as well as the literature and considering the opinions of the other experts, I believe on a balance of probability that this failure of duty of care led to the death of Mrs Gabrielle Davies.'
evidence_index.speaker: Mr Morrison

evidence_index.text: For the foregoing reasons, I will give judgment for the Claimant, in appropriate terms.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mrs Davies experienced tinnitus and a severe headache on 24 February 2015. She was diagnosed with otitis media and prescribed antibiotics. On 25 February 2015, she was in severe pain and suspected meningitis.

focused_exam.neurological: Neck stiffness, GCS 15 initially, later declined

requestables.name: Blood Tests
requestables.description: Includes white cell count and C-reactive protein

reveal.decision_impact: Supports need for urgent antibiotic treatment

reveal.detailed_findings: Imaging performed but findings not detailed in judgment

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with suspected meningitis
decision_points.prompt: When should IV antibiotics be administered?

options.description: Administer IV antibiotics immediately upon suspicion of meningitis
options.clinical_reasoning: Early administration is critical to prevent progression

options.description: Delay antibiotics until after CT scan
options.clinical_reasoning: Delayed treatment increases risk of adverse outcomes

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant delayed antibiotics until after CT scan
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Antibiotics were delayed due to imaging

expected_action_court.description: Antibiotics should have been administered immediately

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in antibiotics led to progression of meningitis

key_findings: Failure to administer IV antibiotics in a timely manner
Causation established - delay contributed to death

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely antibiotics could have prevented death

factual_timeline.event: Patient arrives at Wexham Park Hospital

treatments.outcome: Administered too late to prevent death

allegations.allegation: Failure to administer IV antibiotics timely
